reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Independent policy research values evidence-driven reform, but demands epistemic honesty about unresolved assumptions. S1's equity goal aligns with research-institute commitment to reducing SES-sorting, but the scenario's own evidence assessment — weak-to-moderate — and documented contradictions (SES indicator does not exist; no coaching-resistant instrument demonstrated) make unconditional support indefensible.
reactions.interest: Independent research credibility and prevention of policy failure masquerading as reform
reactions.public_good_frame: Equity gains are only legitimate if mechanisms are evidence-grounded; deploying instruments that do not yet exist, or whose non-gameability is unproven, risks reproducing SES sorting through informal channels while consuming political capital that could support more robust structural change.
reactions.argument: S1 contains three nested failures of evidence discipline: (1) it assumes an SES indicator usable at application time, contradicted by the scenario's own feasibility_checker note that Hungary has no administrative SES register and any proxy would be gameable; (2) it assumes a coaching-resistant assessment can be designed and validated in years 1–3, contradicted by the scenario's own statement that no such instrument is demonstrated and psychometric development timelines are typically 4–6 years; (3) it assumes low-SES underapplication is remediable by outreach, flagged as weak by the scenario's own political_feasibility note that barriers may be structural (transport, school-level gatekeeping) rather than informational. The quota mechanism is mechanically sound, but quotas of what, allocated to whom, rest on instruments that do not exist. Informal selection (interviews, reputation) will reproduce SES sorting in reformed channels if the formal mechanism is perceived as weakened — this is asserted in political_risks but not empirically tested. A research institute cannot recommend deploying unvalidated instruments at scale as if they were ready.
reactions.condition_to_change: S1 would become supportable (move to support) if: (a) a pilot SES-proxy construction showing non-gameability at application time, (b) psychometric validation of a coaching-resistant assessment in a pilot cohort showing effect-size durability across deciles, and (c) qualitative research in 5–10 under-applying settlements isolating whether underapplication stems from information barriers alone. Without these, S1 should be reframed as a three-year evidence-building pilot, not a reform deployment. S1 would move to oppose if implementation proceeds without these validations and informal selection visibly reproduces SES sorting in reformed intake channels.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Independent research values mechanical equity improvements (fewer seats = less early sorting) but demands honesty about offsetting risks and unresolved assumptions. S2's seat-cap mechanism is mechanically sound, but the scenario's own equity_impact acknowledges that residual selective places become more SES-skewed as caps tighten unless paired with S1-type admission reform — and the scenario's own political_risks and uncertainties directly contradict the core assumption that state caps are not offset by non-state maintainers expanding selective intake.
reactions.interest: Preventing false equity claims where structural sorting simply migrates to non-state schools, preserving research credibility in education-system analysis
reactions.public_good_frame: Early sorting is harmful not because selective schools are bad, but because sorting at age 10 concentrates low-SES pupils in parallel tracks before academic stabilisation. If caps on state schools simply displace selective demand to church/foundation schools in the same settlement, sorting persists, equity gains evaporate, and political capital is wasted. Research institutions must flag this risk publicly.
reactions.argument: S2 rests on an assumption directly contradicted by the scenario's own content: that caps on state-maintained schools are not offset by non-state maintainers. The political_risks section states 'Non-state maintainers expand selective intake to absorb displaced demand, defeating the cap' with moderate evidence (school_network_planning; maintainer statistics). The uncertainties section flags 'Will non-state maintainers offset state caps by expanding selective intake?' with low confidence. Yet the implementation plan proceeds as if enforceable caps across maintainer types are achievable in years 1–3, despite this being precisely the unresolved uncertainty. The equity_impact section acknowledges 'Non-state maintainers absorbing displaced selective demand can relocate rather than remove sorting' — a residual-skew mechanism that is mechanical and near-certain given Hungary's church/foundation school network. If this offset occurs, S2 achieves no equity gain, only administrative friction and the political backlash documented in political_risks (prior curtailment attempts triggered sustained organised opposition). A research institute must condition support on prior registry evidence that non-state maintainers will not offset.
reactions.condition_to_change: S2 would become supportable (move to conditional-to-support) if: (a) registry data on selective seats by maintainer type and settlement show that non-state maintainers have not historically expanded intake when state seats contracted, or (b) binding intake negotiations with church/foundation maintainers are concluded in advance, with enforcement mechanisms, and published before legislation. S2 would move to oppose if implementation proceeds without such evidence and selective demand demonstrably migrates to non-state schools, leaving sorting unchanged. S2 would also move to oppose if the parallel-regime transition cost (running two intake systems across a decade) is shown to exceed the Ministry of Finance forecasts, eroding durability.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Independent research supports delayed-selection reform — the international evidence on deferred tracking (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006, Pekkarinen et al. 2009, Jakubowski et al. 2016) is moderate-to-strong on equity gains and no demonstrated loss in mean achievement. However, S3 explicitly acknowledges that its core equity benefit depends on a peer-effect mechanism tagged as weak/contested, and its largest risk — middle-class and church/foundation exit — directly contradicts its own equity_impact assertion that this exit 'does not harm the exiting pupils but can re-concentrate disadvantage' in general schools. Research honesty demands flagging that S3 trades one sorting mechanism (formal selection) for another (residential/maintainer choice), with uncertain net equity effect.
reactions.interest: Defending the integrity of evidence-based reform against false claims of structural change; preventing dismantling of one sorting channel only to legitimise another
reactions.public_good_frame: Delayed selection is justified by international evidence, but only if the structural reform is accompanied by binding constraints on maintainer intake and school choice. If S3 is implemented without these constraints, it becomes formal comprehensive schooling with informal sorting via maintainer flight — this mimics recent Polish reversal (2016–2019) and creates the appearance of reform while leaving equity untouched. Research institutions must demand institutional design that forecloses this drift.
reactions.argument: S3 makes the strongest equity claim of the four scenarios ('Largest expected reduction in early SES sorting') but rests it on two unresolved tensions: (1) The equity gain depends on a peer-effect mechanism tagged as weak/contested in the scenario's own equity_and_social_mobility reference, even though the cross-country delayed-selection association is moderate. This is a gap between plausible aggregate effect and uncertain mechanism — acceptable in research, unacceptable in policy design without prior Hungarian pilot data. (2) The scenario states as an assumption 'Sorting does not simply migrate to school choice between general schools and to non-state maintainers' but then immediately contradicts this in equity_impact: 'middle-class and church/foundation exit to non-state or private schools can relocate sorting rather than remove it... can re-concentrate disadvantage in general schools, partially offsetting the equity gain'. This is not a secondary risk — it is the primary equity threat and it is acknowledged as moderate-to-certain. The implementation plan proposes 'negotiate binding intake rules with church/foundation maintainers to limit sorting migration (timeline: year 1–3)' but does not explain how binding rules differ from non-binding negotiation, which has been attempted repeatedly. Political_feasibility states no durable political window is demonstrated and Poland reversed a comparable reform — S3 is the highest-cost, longest-timeline scenario, yet its political durability is the weakest. A research institute cannot recommend a decade-long structural overhaul that depends on avoiding a migration mechanism the scenario itself identifies as moderate-probability and then asserts without evidence that binding constraints can be enforced.
reactions.condition_to_change: S3 would become supportable (move to conditional-to-support) if: (a) quasi-experimental evidence on residential-choice elasticity to track structure shows that Hungarian non-state-school demand does not increase when comprehensive reform is implemented (synthetic control with Poland/Czech Republic), (b) binding intake agreements with church/foundation maintainers are negotiated and published with enforcement mechanisms before legislation, (c) a multi-year pilot in 5–10 urban and rural districts demonstrates that teacher retraining and diagnostic support can be delivered at scale without degrading academic quality in comprehensive upper grades, and (d) stakeholder interviews with Polish reformers document switching costs of reversal sufficient to deter a future government from dismantling S3. S3 would move to oppose if political ownership does not survive a single electoral cycle (as in Poland) or if maintainer-exit data shows sorting migration offsetting more than 50% of the formal-selection equity gain.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Independent research is committed to evidence-driven policy, but S4 violates a fundamental principle: it proposes offsetting a causal harm (early sorting) by compensation, rather than removing the harm itself. The scenario's own evidence assessment acknowledges this is weak — 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age' — and the assumption is flatly unverifiable. S4 conflates symptom treatment (raising general-school attainment) with structural reform (removing early sorting). Research integrity demands opposing this framing.
reactions.interest: Preventing indefinite entrenchment of a known harm (early selection) under the guise of compensation; defending research credibility against false equity claims
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation is a legitimate policy instrument for mitigating unavoidable inequalities, but early selection at age 10 is not unavoidable — it is a policy choice. If Hungary retains this choice and funds the consequence (low-SES concentration in general schools), it has not solved the problem, it has subsidised it. This legitimises early sorting permanently and diverts resources from structural reform to perpetual management of a self-inflicted wound.
reactions.argument: S4 fails three critical tests of research-informed policy: (1) It assumes compensation can offset early sorting, marked as an assumption in the scenario's own assumption_checker with the note 'unverifiable ex ante; no country evidence exists at Hungary's selection age'. This is not a minor epistemic gap — it is the entire justification for S4. Compensation works downstream (raising general-school attainment), but S4 claims this offsets early sorting (a selection mechanism that concentrates low-SES pupils before academic stabilisation). These operate on different timescales and causal channels. No country has attempted this at Hungary's selection age (10-12 years); Portugal's comparator did not select as early and also did not select as intensively. (2) It invokes the political-durability argument: 'Non-structural interventions are politically easier to sustain than structural change.' This is precisely backwards. Targeted funding (TEIP-style) is the first line cut in fiscal consolidation — the scenario itself notes 'Budget vulnerability: targeted funding is the first line cut in fiscal consolidation, undermining durability'. Structural change (removing early selection) is harder initially but creates a locked-in institutional settlement; compensation is easier initially but chronically vulnerable. S4 is politically fragile, not durable. (3) It forecloses alternatives without justification. S4 is presented as the least-disruptive option, but it is also the lowest-evidence option. Delaying selection to age 14 (S3) has international evidence; reforming admission rules (S1) has mechanical quota logic; phasing seats (S2) has clear implementation steps. S4 has only the hope that compensation works and the historical record that it has not been tried at this selection age. A research institute must oppose recommending an unvalidated mechanism for a known harm when more evidence-grounded alternatives exist.
reactions.condition_to_change: S4 would move to conditional (from oppose) if: (a) a comparison-group evaluation of funded general schools with high-achiever retention tracking showed that SES-outcome gradients narrow by more than 20% within five years, controlling for selection bias, or (b) longitudinal data showed that disadvantaged pupils' attainment gains in funded general schools translate into reduced dropout and improved labour-market outcomes at age 18+. However, even if S4 were to show modest local gains, research integrity would demand that it be paired with structural reform (S1 or S3), not presented as a standalone alternative. S4 alone, as described, rests on an assumption the scenario itself marks as unverifiable and contradicted by international evidence. It would move to strong opposition if implemented without prior pilot evaluation and if targeted funding is cut (as the scenario predicts) before measurable equity gains are documented.
